title: The Child Interpreter
desc: A 12-year-old is translating your cancer disclosure for his grandmother, who speaks only Tashelhit. He paraphrases softly — you suspect he is hiding how serious it is.

law.text: Patients have the right to understandable information about their condition; using minors as primary interpreters in grave discussions raises serious consent and harm concerns.

complications.title: No formal interpreter
complications.premise: Phone line wait is two hours.
complications.liveLine: Charge nurse: "Family says they've always used the grandson — can we just finish rounds?"

complications.title: Son insists
complications.premise: Adult son wants the child to keep today's talk vague.
complications.liveLine: "Mother shouldn't hear everything today — tell the boy to soften it."

complications.title: Boy whispers to you
complications.premise: He asks if grandmother will die this year while she smiles.
complications.liveLine: His voice cracks; he is twelve.

complications.title: Consultant time pressure
complications.premise: Oncology taps the door — consent forms need signing today.

steps.msg: The grandson begins: "She says thank you for being gentle." You had just named metastatic disease. The grandmother looks relieved — too relieved.
steps.q: What do you do in the moment?
steps.hint: Do not proceed with life-altering decisions on child-filtered speech:

choices.label: Pause visit — secure professional interpreter
choices.reaction: The family grumbles about delay — but dignity returns to the room.

choices.label: Continue relying on the child
choices.reaction: Chart later says she 'understood' — she did not, truly.

choices.label: Speak quickly in French only
choices.reaction: Humiliation and fear — she withdraws from care.

choices.label: Dismiss child; deal only with adult son
choices.reaction: Lalla Khadija realizes she's excluded — deep distress.

steps.msg: A video interpreter is connected. The adult son tries to answer questions directed to Lalla Khadija. She holds the boy's hand tightly.
steps.hint: Address the patient; manage family politely but firmly:

choices.label: Direct questions to Lalla Khadija through interpreter
choices.reaction: Slowly, she owns the words — fear and clarity together.

choices.label: Let the son filter all answers
choices.reaction: She signs forms nodding — uncertainty remains.

choices.label: Postpone serious talk until outpatient
choices.reaction: Lost window — recurrence complications shock her in ER.

steps.msg: The boy cries in the hall: "I didn't want to lie — they made me." Social work offers counseling. The family asks you not to document "family coercion."
steps.q: How do you document and follow up?
steps.hint: Protect the child, be accurate, offer support:

choices.label: Accurate chart + social work for child + family meeting
choices.reaction: Painful chart — but integrity and help align.

choices.label: Sanitize chart to appease family
choices.reaction: Next team repeats the interpreter mistake.

choices.label: Document child as "unreliable historian"
choices.reaction: The boy believes medicine punished him.

debrief.summary: Children should not bear grave diagnostic translation. Professional interpreters, direct patient address, and honest documentation uphold autonomy and shield minors from impossible emotional duties.
points: Convenience never justifies implied consent through a child's paraphrase.
Family cultural preferences are respected by timing and tone — not by hiding truth from a capable adult.
Health systems owe timely interpreter access; improvised fixes offload harm onto patients and kids.
Support both elder and child — ethical pediatric concern extends to the grandchildren in the room.
